projects.title: Retail KPI Storytelling
projects.impact: Delivered a repeatable analytics path: RFM segments for customer prioritization, YTD/MTD KPI views for recurring review, and charts framed for decisions rather than exploratory notebooks alone.
projects.problem: The retail dataset needed a clear view of who buys, what sells, and how performance moves month-to-month - not a pile of raw retail tables.
projects.summary: Formation Data & Business Intelligence project on a retail dataset covering Sales, Product, Customer, and Store tables. I built the full analytical path from relational modeling and advanced SQL through cleaning, RFM segmentation, time-based KPIs, and visual storytelling for business questions.
projects.tagline: Sales, customers, and stores turned into RFM segments and YTD/MTD KPIs.
projects.approach: Modeled the four-table retail schema, wrote advanced SQL (joins, CTEs, window functions, views), cleaned and transformed sales data in Python, built RFM customer segments, and designed YTD/MTD KPI views with Matplotlib and Seaborn so trends were scannable.
highlights: Queried a 4-table retail schema with joins, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, views, and stored procedures.
Built RFM customer segments and Year-to-Date / Month-to-Date KPI views from cleaned sales data.
Used pandas and NumPy for exploration, then Matplotlib and Seaborn to present trends teams could act on - hierarchy of KPIs first, detail second.
projects.roleBody: Owned modeling, advanced SQL, transformation, and KPI storytelling across Sales, Product, Customer, and Store tables.
projects.goal: Turn a multi-table retail dataset into a clear, repeatable view of who buys, what sells, and how performance moves month to month.

projects.title: Trusted Test Data Reporting
projects.impact: Moved reporting from ad-hoc manual collation to a repeatable JIRA-backed workflow with clearer anomaly visibility and faster turnaround for cross-functional reviews.
projects.problem: Cross-functional engineering teams needed faster, trustworthy answers from high-volume test and diagnostic data - manual collation slowed decisions and hid anomalies.
projects.summary: At Capgemini Engineering and KPIT Engineering, I analyzed and validated large Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, automated collection workflows in JIRA, and delivered clear reports for hardware, software, and systems teams.
projects.tagline: Large validation datasets cleaned, tracked, and turned into decisions.
projects.approach: Validated large-scale HiL and system test datasets, automated data collection and defect tracking in JIRA, and wrote Python scripts to generate result data and reporting for hardware, software, and systems teams.
highlights: Validated large-scale Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, surfacing performance trends and anomalies for engineering teams.
Automated data collection and defect-tracking workflows in JIRA to improve traceability and cut analysis turnaround.
Built Python scripts to generate and analyze result data, improving reporting accuracy for hardware, software, and systems teams.
projects.roleBody: Analyzed and validated large Hardware-in-the-Loop and system test datasets, automated collection and defect tracking, and built clearer anomaly visibility for engineering teams.
projects.goal: Give cross-functional engineering teams faster, trustworthy answers from high-volume test and diagnostic data.

projects.title: Chetek Sports | Tennis Referee Companion
projects.impact: A build-ready UX package: research artifacts, a sharp persona, process wireframes, and a high-fidelity French system spanning onboarding, licence trust gates, planning and assignments, live scoring, rules learning with a quiz, and post-match reporting. The handoff let engineering build from decisions, not guesses.
projects.problem: Mid-point, an arbiter has seconds to apply the right rule. Instead they juggle bulky physical rulebooks, static ITF/ATP/WTA PDFs, and thin third-party summaries, none of them built for a phone held courtside. Every extra tap or page flip is hesitation in front of players, and hesitation reads as doubt.
projects.summary: Vivez votre match comme les pros. Chetek Sports is a native iOS companion I designed for French tennis arbiters, the people running the match from the chair. I took it from lived friction and research through persona, wireframes, and a high-fidelity French system that covers the whole match day: onboarding, licence trust, assignments, live scoring, rules learning, and post-match reporting. Research and hi-fi by me; development by Ilyes.
projects.tagline: UX for tennis arbiters: research, French flows, and a full match-day system for the chair.
projects.approach: I started from the client arbiter's real mid-match friction rather than a sports-app template. I mapped the competitive gap, ran a survey on lookup speed and offline need, and shaped persona Walid around one primary seat. From there I moved through low-fi wireframes and mid-fi court scoring, then into a broader hi-fi platform so the product owned the full match day, not just the score.
highlights: Grounded the product in a working arbiter's mid-match friction, not a generic sports brief.
Diagnosed why official rulebooks, PDFs, and thin apps all fail under live time pressure.
Persona Walid Chetek: a Marseille arbiter-coach who needs speed, offline access, and clear interpretation.
Carried the story from wireframes to mid-fi court scoring, then a full hi-fi system for the official seat.
Kept one calm French voice across auth, planning, scoring, and rules so the product felt official.
projects.roleBody: I owned research and product UX end to end for a freelance brief: competitive scan, survey synthesis, persona, information architecture, user flows, and high-fidelity French UI. The brief came from a tennis arbiter who lived the problem on court, so every decision could be checked against a real user. Ilyes handled development from my handoff.
projects.goal: Give French-speaking tennis arbiters a companion they trust mid-match: faster orientation under pressure, offline-minded thinking, and match tools from tirage au sort through live scoring, all in one official voice.

metrics.label: Design arc

metrics.label: Primary user

sections.title: Introduction
sections.body:
Chetek Sports is a native iOS companion for tennis arbiters in France. The brief came from a working arbiter who was still juggling rulebooks, PDFs, and thin tools while the point was live, so the product had a real user from day one.

The ambition was not another scoreboard. It was a calm, official-feeling companion that owns the whole match day, from the moment an arbiter opens the app to the report they file after the last point.

MY ROLE — I owned research and product UX end to end: competitive scan, survey synthesis, persona, information architecture, flows, and high-fidelity French UI. Ilyes handled development from my handoff.

sections.title: Problem
sections.body:
PROBLEM — When a call is contested, the arbiter needs the right rule now, in front of players, not after flipping through three sources.

EXAMPLE — Physical rulebooks are bulky and slow. Official PDFs are complete but awkward to search mid-point. Switching between a book and phone tabs breaks eye contact and rhythm exactly when composure matters most.

IMPACT — Slow, uncertain lookups cost the arbiter authority. Errors creep in, players push harder, and the match loses its rhythm.

PROBLEM — No dedicated tennis-arbiter companion combined official depth with real mobile speed.

EXAMPLE — ITF, ATP, and WTA materials are authoritative but static. Third-party summaries are thin and unofficial. General officiating apps rarely handle tennis edge cases like tie-breaks, ball changes, or side switches.

IMPACT — That gap was the opening: official seriousness with phone speed, designed specifically for the chair.

images.alt: Research slide on why the arbiter companion exists
images.caption: I opened with the why, framed from lived courtside friction rather than a feature wishlist.

images.alt: Market research slide with claim and problem
images.caption: Market view: arbiters need speed and clarity under live pressure, and nothing served that well.

images.alt: Competitive analysis of rulebooks and officiating apps
images.caption: Competitive scan: existing tools offered depth without speed, or speed without real tennis depth.

sections.title: Goals
sections.body:
BUSINESS GOALS — Deliver a companion arbiters trust mid-match, and hand engineering a sharp, build-ready package from research through hi-fi.

Move beyond a static rule PDF to owning match-day control: orientation, setup, scoring, and learning in one coherent product voice.

USER GOALS — Faster orientation under pressure, offline-minded thinking for dead venue connectivity, and tools from tirage au sort through live scoring, with no fan clutter competing for attention.

A calm French interface that feels official on the chair instead of like a generic sports template.

sections.title: Impact
sections.body:
This was a freelance arc delivered in phases: research, low-fi wireframes, mid-fi scoring exploration, then a fuller hi-fi system for the official seat. The measures below are honest scope markers for a solo research-to-hi-fi brief, not invented adoption or conversion numbers.

The real outcome is a package an engineer could build from with confidence: every screen ties back to a research input, a persona need, or an explicit match-day job.

sections.title: Our Users
sections.body:
Walid Chetek is 42 and based in Marseille: five years as an arbiter, ten as a coach. He is comfortable on a phone yet still stuck with bulky rulebooks when the point is hot.

JOB TO BE DONE — When a rule is contested mid-match, I want a calm, searchable companion so I can decide quickly without breaking the rhythm of the court.

He asked for offline access, quick reference, scenario learning, and even a digital coin toss. Anchoring to one real seat shaped the product far more than any generic sports template, and it gave me a clear test for every later feature: does this help Walid decide faster on court?

images.alt: Persona card for Walid Chetek tennis arbiter
images.caption: Persona first: one primary seat, one clear job, a real test for every feature that followed.

sections.title: Process: Sketches
sections.body:
I mapped the full match day in low-fidelity wireframes before touching visual design: login, competition pick, the rencontres schedule, team lineups, match-code entry, tirage au sort, match parameters, the pre-match recap, and the first court-scoring ideas.

At this stage I cared about structure and French microcopy jobs, not polish. Seeing the whole flow in grey boxes is where I cut taps between ready and live, and where the coin-toss and side-selection logic got pinned down.

images.caption: Login and entry, kept to one job.
Choose a competition or drop into a match code.
Rencontres: the day as a scannable schedule.
Team lineups from singles through doubles.
Quick match-code path for one-off matches.
Tirage au sort: toss, serve, and side in one place.
Match parameters: format, ball change, tie-break rules.
Pre-match recap to confirm before the first point.
An early edge case: the tie-break points modal.
First court-scoring idea, tap targets over chrome.

sections.title: Process: Early designs
sections.body:
Mid-fidelity screens tightened the auth path and proved the core bet: scoring as muscle memory. A court you can tap, with Ace, Filet, Faute, and F. de pied within reach while the point is still alive.

This is where the LiveScore companion stopped being a diagram and started to feel real, with the active side flipping as play switches ends. Testing it here, before hi-fi, kept the later platform expansion grounded in a scoring model that already worked.

images.caption: Welcome, setting the calm entry tone.
Login without friction before a match.
Register, kept short.
Forgot password, one clear recovery start.
Verification step in the same voice.
Success state that closes the recovery loop.
Light scoring theme for bright courts.
The core bet: a tappable court, actions in reach.
A live scoring state mid-game.
Active side flips as players switch ends.

sections.title: Process: Listening
sections.body:
Alongside the client arbiter, I drew on the difficulties officials share when applying rules live. The survey pressed on current resources, lookup speed, unclear interpretations, must-have features, and how critical offline access is when venue connectivity dies.

That input did not sit in a slide. It set clear priorities: calm auth, schedule orientation, scoring as muscle memory, and, later, rules learning that lives beside the official documents.

images.alt: User survey questions for tennis arbiters
images.caption: Survey prompts that ranked speed, clarity, and offline need over nice-to-haves.

sections.title: Looks new on the chair
sections.body:
The final system opens with court light and a clear promise for the people running the match. The splash and onboarding welcome set an official, confident voice before any form asks for attention.

First impressions carry trust here: an arbiter should feel this app belongs on the chair with them.

images.caption: Splash: brand and promise in one calm frame.
Onboarding welcome that speaks to the arbiter, not a fan.

sections.title: Onboarding the job
sections.body:
Onboarding teaches the job in short, single-purpose beats: planning, scoring, availability, then a setup-complete confirmation. Each screen owns one idea so the arbiter learns what the product does before match day, not during it.

Keeping onboarding this disciplined mirrored a core principle from the whole system: one screen, one job.

images.caption: Planning: see the day at a glance.
Scoring: the core action, previewed.
Availability: tell the system when you can officiate.
Setup complete: ready for match day.

sections.title: Auth that stays calm
sections.body:
Auth had to be boring in the best way. Login, register, and verification stay in one calm French voice so arbiters never fight forms before a match.

I treated recovery and verification as a single considered path rather than a pile of disconnected screens, so the tone stays official even when something goes wrong.

images.caption: Login: fast, familiar, low friction.
Sign up: only what the role needs.
Verification in the same official voice.

sections.title: Trust before tools
sections.body:
For an officiating product, credibility is the feature. Home can stay read-only until the licence is verified, so the app never lets an unverified account act as an official.

The pending state and verification path make that trust explicit. Blocked actions are shown as pending, not hidden behind buttons that quietly fail, which respects the arbiter and the integrity of the match.

images.caption: Home in a pending state: honest about what is not unlocked yet.
Licence verification: the trust gate before real tools open.

sections.title: The day is assignments
sections.body:
Tournament days are logistics before they are points. This path keeps the arbiter oriented: calendar view, full assignment detail, venue and role notes, and a pre-match readiness screen.

Pulling all of that into one flow means an arbiter can arrive prepared, knowing the court, the role, and the context, before the first serve.

images.caption: Planning calendar: where the day starts.
Assignment detail: match, role, and venue in one place.
Pre-match readiness before walking to the chair.

sections.title: Live on court
sections.body:
Live scoring is the loudest idea in the product, and the one I validated earliest. Large point actions, a running log of recent events, and a match summary that closes the loop after the final point.

The layout puts the court and its tap targets first and chrome second, so scoring stays muscle memory even under pressure. It is the direct descendant of the mid-fi court I tested in the process phase.

images.caption: Live scoring: court and actions first, chrome second.
Match summary that closes the loop after the last point.

sections.title: Rules in the pocket
sections.body:
Rules become searchable in the pocket, with recent updates surfaced and a quiz path for scenario learning. Instead of living in a separate PDF tab, interpretation sits right beside the official documents.

This is where the product answers the original problem head on: the right rule, and the reasoning behind it, available in seconds without breaking the arbiter's focus.

images.caption: Reglements: official documents, searchable on the phone.
Quiz question: scenario learning, not passive reading.
Quiz results that reinforce the tricky interpretations.

sections.title: After the match
sections.body:
The match is not done at the last point. Post-match reporting closes the day, and a clear confirmation makes the handoff to organizers feel finished and official.

Treating reporting as part of the core loop, rather than an afterthought, keeps the product honest to the arbiter's real responsibilities.

images.caption: Report confirmation: the day handed off cleanly.

sections.title: Support when stuck
sections.body:
When the product or the rules still leave a question, help and live chat stay within reach. For an official tool, reachable support is itself part of trust on match day.

It is the safety net behind the confidence the rest of the system projects.

images.caption: Help center for the common questions.
Live chat for the moments that need a person.

sections.title: What we learned
sections.body:
Designing for one primary seat clarified everything. Every time a spectator or generic-sports feature tempted its way in, the persona test cut it: it did not help Walid decide faster on court.

French microcopy was not decoration, it was trust. Auth, planning, scoring, and rules all had to sound like the same official voice, or the product would feel unofficial.

Process artifacts earned their place. Validating the tappable court in mid-fi is what made the later platform expansion honest instead of theatrical, because the riskiest interaction was already proven.

sections.title: Future
sections.body:
After handoff, the most honest next step is arbiter testing in live tournaments: how deep offline rule lookup really needs to go, whether scenario learning changes on-court confidence, and which assignment and scoring edge cases only surface when the point is hot.

Any post-launch iteration should follow real match-day use and measured behaviour, not more screens added for their own sake.

projects.nextSteps: Test with arbiters in live tournaments: offline rule-lookup depth, scenario learning's effect on confidence, and the scoring and assignment edge cases that only appear when the point is hot. Let real match-day behaviour, not screen count, drive the next iteration.

projects.title: Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisia
projects.impact: 290 members on the official FoF network; hosted Tunisia Config Watch Party 2026 and recurring meetups; speaking at DevFest, GDG MENAT Summit, and Women Techmakers.
projects.problem: Tunisia lacked an official Figma-affiliated community space for designers and builders to learn together in public.
projects.summary: I founded and lead Friends of Figma (FoF) Tunisiaiaia, listed on the official Friends of Figma network as Tunisia’s chapter for Africa. As Figma Community Advocate and organizer, I grow a local space for designers and builders through meetups, Config watch parties, speaker programs, and learning in public - the same communication craft I bring to data work.
projects.tagline: Official Friends of Figma Tunisia chapter - 290 members and growing.
projects.approach: Founded and organized FoF Tunisiaiaia: meetups, Config watch parties, speaker programs, and partnerships with WTM and GDG - connecting design practice with clear public storytelling.

Hosted Tunisia Config Watch Party 2026 and meetups such as Vibe Right with Figma and Ramadhan Bytes: Design Edition.
Connect design and data storytelling through talks at DevFest, GDG MENAT Summit 2025, and Women Techmakers.

aboutPage.bio: I'm Amani, a Data Analyst in Ariana, Tunisia. I analyze complex datasets and build KPI narratives with SQL and Python, with a designer's eye for clarity.

vision.body: I have a clear plan for growth in the coming years: deepen analytical craft while continuing to lead with design clarity. I want to shape narratives from data and from product experience, make sharper decisions, and drive design thinking across teams. I aspire to lead, foster learning in public, and build work that people trust and enjoy using.

workProcess.body: Understand business goals and project requirements with clients, then run user research including interviews, surveys, analytics, and competitor analysis. Identify pain points and motivations through persona creation.
workProcess.title: Research & discovery

workProcess.body: Map content and navigation with sitemaps and user flows. Prioritize features that align with user needs and product goals so the structure stays usable and purposeful.
workProcess.title: Information architecture & planning

workProcess.body: Craft low-fidelity wireframes to shape layout and functionality, then bring designs to life with interactive prototypes for testing and validation.
workProcess.title: Wireframing & prototyping

workProcess.body: Enhance the interface with branding, typography, and color while keeping accessibility and consistency. Add micro-interactions when they improve clarity and polish.
workProcess.title: UI design & visual development

workProcess.body: Conduct usability testing, gather feedback, and refine wireframes, prototypes, and visuals based on real user interactions.
workProcess.title: Testing & iteration

workProcess.body: Prepare clear handoff for engineering so the validated experience can be built with consistent components, states, and flows.
workProcess.title: Handoff & implementation

workProcess.body: Track behavior with analytics and heatmaps, gather feedback, and keep improving engagement through continuous design updates.
workProcess.title: Post-launch evaluation & optimization
